title: The Ethics of Algorithmic Visibility
excerpt: For as long as I have been online, I have believed that social and digital media could function as infrastructure for collective power. I have dreamed of a world where…
body_html:
For as long as I have been online, I have believed that social and digital media could function as infrastructure for collective power. I have dreamed of a world where people can recognize themselves in an issue, understand how to participate, and find others doing the same work with ease. But I also interrogate that belief, and how it holds up against the systems that shape how information moves in reality. I find that my dreams are fundamentally at odds with the companies that built the systems that distribute information, systems deliberately designed to optimize for attention patterns that undermine how complex, trust-based learning develops over time.

Platform design is at the center of how content travels, because the same systems that surface content also shape what kinds of messages can survive long enough to be understood. The recent jury verdict holding Meta and Google liable for harm tied to addictive design makes clear that platforms are deliberately structured to distribute information in ways that reinforce compulsive engagement.

In March 2026, A California jury evaluated a case involving a young user who began using YouTube at age six and Instagram at age nine. The first trial of its kind to focus on platform design as a source of harm, this changes the conversation around building collective power online as we reconcile how platforms determine what is repeatedly shown, amplified, and reinforced over time.

Distribution operates through accumulation, where repeated signals train both the user and the model. Platforms require consistent input to classify and expand reach. Meaning, visibility is produced, not guaranteed, through sustained interaction between content and the platform.

The ethical question begins inside the attention loop. Algorithms reinforce the behaviors that sustain attention, where emotional intensity, simplicity, and immediacy generate stronger signals than context-heavy or slower-moving information. Across platform data, content supply has expanded faster than attention capacity due to AI-generated production, creating denser feeds that require stronger filtering and prediction platforms. In turn, platforms prioritize signals that can be measured quickly such as watch time, completion, and interaction velocity. As density increases, distribution thresholds tighten, and the kinds of content that travel most efficiently are those that resolve quickly for both the platform and the viewer. Short-form video shows how this compression operates across the market, where publishing volume increased by over 70% while reach per post declined. More content enters the platform without expanding the amount of attention available to absorb it. This dynamic creates a translation gap for work that depends on cumulative understanding, sustained trust, and layered context. Climate, clean energy, and advocacy topics don’t always compress cleanly into high-velocity signals, and the language required to explain them often loses coherence as it moves through platforms designed for rapid classification and retention.

As content volume increases and attention remains fixed, the threshold for visibility rises. Clarity becomes a classification advantage, because platforms are more likely to distribute content that can be mapped cleanly to a topic. Ambiguity or complexity reduces the probability of expansion, which reinforces the attention loop, by simplifying narratives to travel further and shape audience expectations. This loop continues to shape how people encounter issues for the first time. Language tied to lived experience carries more weight in early attention, while institutional framing organizes the work internally but does not always translate into participation externally, creating a translation gap between recognition and entry.

The recent Meta and Youtube verdicts brings the translation gap into the conversation around platform design, because if platforms can be evaluated based on the behavioral outcomes they produce, then the distribution platforms shaping public understanding of social issues become part of that same ethical landscape.

The ethics of algorithmic visibility emerge from how that landscape is constructed, each decision about framing, pacing, and format influences whether someone can locate themselves inside the issue, whether they can trust what they are seeing, and whether there is a visible way to act on it. In this environment, ethical strategy starts with designing language and structure that can survive inside these platforms while preserving the pathways to understanding and participation that collective power depends on.

01The Ensh*ttification

We must also consider the economic incentives shaping these platforms. Cory Doctorow has described the results of these incentives as Ensh*ttification, where platforms stop optimizing for user experience and switch to extracting increasing value from attention. Over time, this lifecycle has reshaped how feeds prioritize and distribute content.

As economic incentives grow, the same mechanisms that drive engagement also function as extraction, prioritizing content that sustains attention even when it compresses complexity. This explains why we have platforms engineered to create compulsive behavior and reinforce content that aligns with economic incentives. Users are left to fend for themselves across platforms that make it impossible to meaningfully distinguish between what users would chose to engage with and what has been engineered to hold their attention.

The Translation Gap and The Distortion Norm

The translation gap is formed when ideas are compressed as they enter platforms. Translation allows a message to align with the pace and structure of a feed while maintaining its internal coherence. Distortion is when the message itself is reshaped to fit the signals a platform rewards. This becomes the norm when platforms reward high-velocity reaction over retention.

Every platform imposes constraints that require translation: short-form video compresses time, feeds prioritize immediacy, and ranking platforms depend on early interaction signals. This does require that information be structured in ways that can be recognized quickly. This process can still preserve the pathways someone needs to follow the issue further, especially when it connects to deeper context across formats. Distortion becomes normalized when the structure of the platform determines the substance of the message, which appears in practices that generate signals without building understanding, including rage-bait, performative controversy, and false urgency.

Rage-bait aligns directly with how ranking platforms operate, because replies, shares, and rapid interaction loops act as distribution inputs. Conversation signals increasingly influence reach across platforms, which creates a direct incentive to produce content that generates immediate response even if that response does not deepen comprehension. The same pattern extends into performative controversy and false urgency, where stakes are elevated to accelerate attention cycles, allowing content to pass through algorithmic thresholds more quickly, while the underlying issue becomes secondary to the reaction it produces, which shifts the role of communication from informing participation to sustaining visibility. Repeated exposure to reactive framing establishes expectations about how an issue should be encountered, which then influences how future content is interpreted, shared, and engaged with, reinforcing an attention loop where simplification becomes the dominant entry point.

Platforms that reward retention and interaction at scale create conditions where distortion can operate as an effective growth strategy, even as it narrows the range of understanding available to the audience. The consequence appears in the type of community that forms, where entry points built on reaction tend to produce participation that remains at the level of response, limiting the ability to carry people from awareness into sustained involvement with complex issues. For organizations working on long-term challenges, the way an issue is introduced determines whether someone can locate themselves within it and whether the next step is visible, which ties participation in your broader work directly to how your language maps across platforms and access.

An ethical strategy operates within these constraints by adapting in support of recognition while preserving the structure needed for action, ensuring that translation maintains coherence rather than reorganizing the issue around the signals required for visibility.

Vanity metrics, trust, and how to talk to leadership

This becomes visible in how metrics are interpreted once content enters reporting structures, because likes, views, and reach are often treated as outcomes when they are actually traces of different kinds of behavior occurring inside a platform that measures interaction more easily than understanding.

Each metric carries a distinct function tied to how platforms organize visibility. Where a like reflects recognition within an existing network, a share or send reflects relevance strong enough to pass into someone else’s immediate context, and a comment reflects an entry into conversation that can carry agreement, disagreement, or curiosity. Overall, these signals describe movement inside the feed instead of comprehension outside the platform.

Platform design separates interaction from interpretation, allowing content to accumulate high visibility through signals that don’t require someone to fully process what they are seeing. This is intensified in environments where attention is fragmented and exposure happens in rapid sequence, which reinforces the translation gap between what travels and what lands. Platforms also increasingly rely on behavioral signals like replies, dwell time, and engagement probability to determine distribution, which means the metrics available to organizations are optimized for what the platform can observe rather than what the organization ultimately needs to know about participation or understanding.

The most useful way to frame metrics is in terms of the type of behavior they capture. Network signals such as likes indicate support among people already proximate to the organization. Distribution signals such as reach indicate how far content traveled through recommendation platforms. Neither of these alone confirms whether someone can locate themselves within the issue or understand what to do next. This is where platform logic reinforces the need for translation. Platforms differentiate between content that circulates within known networks and content that expands into new audiences, and those two forms of movement produce different kinds of feedback that cannot be collapsed into a single measure of success.

Ethical visibility appears when these metrics are used as proxies for trust or action, because equating interaction with impact obscures whether the communication is actually enabling participation, which is the outcome most mission-driven organizations are trying to achieve. Reframe metrics as signals tied to specific roles within the platform, where likes indicate recognition, shares indicate peer-level relevance, comments indicate conversational entry, and actions such as clicks, signups, replies, or event participation indicate movement beyond the platform into forms of engagement that require higher levels of understanding. This situates vanity metrics within a broader structure which is advantageous because they remain necessary inputs for distribution and provide early feedback on how content is being received. To align vanity metrics with participation, shift from how content performed inside the platform to how communication functions across platforms, where the central question becomes whether the audience can recognize their place within the issue and whether the next step is visible once that recognition occurs.

What platforms might do next

Across platforms, the ethics of algorithmic visibility continues to resolve through user behavior, where attention follows what feels immediately legible within a feed and trust forms through proximity to people, communities, or experiences that already carry meaning, which creates an environment where discovery, advertising, and participation operate as a continuous surface.

If that direction continues, social impact communication may encounter a different set of incentives, where clarity, consistency, and relational trust become more aligned with platform objectives, yet that alignment remains uncertain and dependent on how platforms interpret safety in relation to engagement.

What remains consistent across these changes is the condition that strategy must account for how people enter the work, because entry determines whether recognition occurs and whether participation becomes possible, which means designing for reach without recognition limits the ability to build sustained engagement.

Visibility is just a starting point that must connect to clarity, where the goal is not only to appear in the feed but to remain understandable within it, allowing someone to locate themselves inside the issue and see a path forward that extends beyond the initial interaction.

If the mechanics that drive virality are the same ones being examined as sources of harm, then ethical visibility must account for how to build trust and collective power within platforms that were not designed for either, and the answer will depend on whether communication can remain legible, actionable, and grounded as it moves through the environments that shape what people see and how they respond.

title: The Future Is Community-Led
excerpt: Remember being a kid at your local playground? Where I grew up in New York, we’d take the Subway across the city to experience different parks. A slide? A tire swing?…
body_html:
Remember being a kid at your local playground? Where I grew up in New York, we’d take the Subway across the city to experience different parks. A slide? A tire swing? These were hot commodities in kindergarten and I was lucky to have the time and opportunity to explore them. All public goods are like this, and it’s a barrier to government trust and collective action that we need to address. There are hidden costs to access that we ignore when we have it, which makes it harder to expand opportunity because the people with access rarely have to think about how it works or how to extend it.

If we entertain the fact that sustainability, clean energy, and climate are only accessible to some, because it allows climate and clean energy public goods to proliferate in small ways, then we will continue to fail to address the largest barriers to the clean energy transition. We must demand a community-led energy transition for climate and clean energy. A community-led movement will both center communities in the benefits of climate investments and promote mass adoption on a local level.

Adam Elman, Director of Sustainability for Europe, the Middle East and Africa at Google, said, “the era of vague, sweeping corporate sustainability pledges is over.” The brands that are adapting, are messaging around tangible value and focusing on affordability.

Campaigns grounded in real, material outcomes hold attention longer and build credibility faster because they give people something concrete to respond to. And increasingly, with the transition from “climate change” messaging to weather and energy bills, we are seeing that people are engaging with and recognizing climate in the way they always have: through their community and lived experience.

I wanted to build a tool that could test what I was seeing in digital spaces against real data. I built what I now call the Incantation Crafter, a practical way to help clients and paid subscribers find language that actually works across platforms. So, I pulled data from 320 climate organization websites. Then I mapped that language across Google Ads, YouTube, and Bluesky, using the same terms and the same weighting system across each environment. I expected relative consistency and the same terms I’ve seen across the climate movement for years. What I saw was a gap between how organizations describe the work and how people experience it, and I want to explain why that has me optimistic for the future.

01What does the language say?

For this analysis, I analyzed how language functions in practice for climate, clean energy, and advocacy organizations. I focused on “justice” as a core search term because it sits at the center of how the climate movement defines itself. Environmental justice has become one of the primary frameworks for organizing climate work, shaping how organizations describe their mission, their values, and their priorities. It carries historical weight, ties together climate, health, and economic conditions, and signals who the work is meant to serve. At the same time, it is a term that does not always travel cleanly across platforms or into public-facing language, which makes it a useful way to trace how meaning holds, shifts, or drops off as it moves from institutions into lived experience.

Across 329 organizations, justice shows up in 50.5% of cases somewhere across category, keywords, or internal phrasing. Most of that sits at the category level, where 47.7% of organizations use justice to define the work.

Environmental Justice accounts for 126 organizations, or 38.3% of the dataset. Over 50% of organizations reference frontline communities as a target audience.

I also conducted a weighted analysis to look for patterns across organizations. Across 867 keyword rows, 41.5% connect to justice through direct language or category association.

The signal sits in how language is organized. At the phrase level, 12.6% of instances include “justice,” and phrases containing it account for 15% of total words. Justice appears in just 3.7% of total words but anchors 41.5% of weighted keyword structure

Across the dataset, justice defines the work broadly, but isn’t consistently shaping how people engage in practice. That gap separates definition from participation.

When environmental justice connects to a clear way to participate, it creates infrastructure to bring people to the work. Without that connection, it stays at the level of framing. This is the gap between language that signals alignment internally and language that can actually drive participation externally.

02What do platforms reveal?

On Bluesky in particular, climate language clusters around immediate experience. Weather, local conditions, direct impact. You see fragments that point to lived reality, grounded in what people are actually dealing with. Less than ~5% of extracted phrases align with institutional language like justice or policy. But when expanded to include weather, seasons, and local conditions, experience-based language increases by up to 10–20x.

The language stays close to experience because that is where recognition happens. People are encountering climate through heat, smoke, flooding, storms, and disruptions to daily life.

YouTube maintains higher alignment with institutional language than Bluesky, but still reflects movement toward explanatory and experience-adjacent framing

Organizational language carries structure and defines the work in a way that makes sense internally and across institutions, but it does not always translate into how people first come into contact with the issue. Platform language decides whether something applies to the audience, whether it feels relevant, and whether it is worth paying attention to.

Language tied to lived experience carries more weight in early attention, while institutional language continues to organize the work internally. The gap between the two defines how people encounter the issue, and whether they recognize a way to take action.

03Where does the gap lead?

The climate movement has spent the last decade building scale through abstraction. Most of the large climate investments in the U.S. assume people can spend money in order to save money. That shapes who can participate. Clean energy tax credits, for example, skew toward households with enough income and enough flexibility to front the cost and wait for reimbursement. If you can’t absorb that gap, you’re effectively locked out, even if the program is technically available to you.

Programs that are designed to reach lower-income communities follow this pattern. The IRA’s low-income solar bonus pushed tens of thousands of projects into qualifying areas, but the qualification was tied to geography rather than community beneficiaries. Most projects met the requirement by being located in a low-income community, without a mechanism to ensure the savings actually reached residents.

When the language of a movement stops matching lived experience, participation thins out. The issue is recognition. People need to see where they fit inside the work. Right now, that path has an income barrier. The result is a movement that can describe its goals clearly while leaving the point of entry less defined. This pattern shows up in how access is structured, and it clarifies how participation is distributed.

If the largest climate investment in U.S. history is easier to access with existing capital, that shapes who enters first and how quickly benefits are felt. You get deployment without distribution. The infrastructure exists, but the benefits don’t land evenly. This is where the transition starts to split. It can scale quickly while still concentrating early advantages among people who already have the ability to participate because the design assumes a baseline of access that doesn’t exist.

You can see that tension again when it moves from households to state policy. New York built one of the most aggressive climate frameworks in the country, and now it’s running into the same constraint at a larger scale. The state is trying to reconcile emissions targets with cost of living pressure, and the response has been to delay timelines, adjust targets, and reconsider how progress is measured. The policy requires costs to show up first: carbon pricing, electrification, infrastructure buildout. All of it increases costs in the near term and distributes benefits later. Even under more optimistic scenarios, households would feel higher energy costs before they see savings come back through incentives or system changes.

If participation requires absorbing short-term cost, then participation will follow income. At the household level, that shows up in who claims tax credits. At the state level, it shows up in whether a policy can hold under pressure. The transition can never reach meaningful scale while access remains conditional, especially when the programs designed to expand participation still rely on the same assumptions about cost and entry.

04How about attention and trust?

Looking across all four datasets, the pattern resolves into something consistent with how distribution already works. Justice holds a central role inside climate communications. Once that language moves into public platforms, it spreads out, and other forms of language start to carry more weight. Experience, place, and immediacy begin to do more of the work.

This aligns with how platforms are actually ranking content now. Feeds are layering proximity into that system, learning where you are and anchoring attention in physical context.

Attention follows that behavior. In the YouGov data, heavy social media users skew younger, with Gen Z making up the largest share, and they move through content, ads, and discovery as one continuous environment. Nearly 50% of users discover products through social and digital ads, but peer recommendations remain the leading driver of trust. This reinforces why language grounded in lived experience travels further than institutional framing

We see this play out in how language travels. Content that connects to lived experience holds attention longer because it fits into how people already process information. It gives someone a way to place themselves inside what they are seeing.

Language that stays at the level of definition organizes, it allows signaling across institutions. Language that connects to place and experience will determine who recognizes themselves inside it, and who decides to take part.

05Where are we going?

This is what the tool was built to surface. The Incantation Crafter tracks how language carries across systems, where it holds its meaning, where it starts to thin, and where it needs to be translated to stay legible. It makes visible something that is usually felt but not measured, which is how far language can travel before it stops connecting.

This dataset makes it clear that the challenge is where language is expected to support structure. Language is carrying too much of the burden for access, for trust, and for participation, and it cannot solve those gaps on its own.

The organizations that will adapt fastest are the ones that treat language as part of infrastructure. Those who designed messaging alongside access, distribution, and participation from the beginning. That is where alignment starts to show up across platforms, and where recognition becomes easier.

There is also a change happening in how credibility is built. Institutional authority is no longer enough to carry language across environments. People are looking for signals that feel close to their own experience, which means credibility is being formed at the level of community, not just at the level of organization. That changes how influence travels, how information spreads, and how movements grow.

It also changes how success should be measured. Reach alone does not tell you whether language is working. Engagement alone does not tell you whether someone can act on what they are seeing. The more useful question is whether someone can locate themselves inside the work, and whether there is a clear way to take part once they do. That is where language and structure finally meet.

The gap you see across this analysis is already reshaping how the transition unfolds. As cost, proximity, and trust continue to define entry points, the center of gravity will keep moving toward community-level experience. That is where recognition happens first, where participation becomes possible, and where momentum actually builds. This is why models that remove upfront cost and localize access are foundational. Community-based financing, direct pay mechanisms, community benefit agreements, and community-led project design are already showing a different entry point. These programs reduce upfront barriers and make participation visible at the local level. When those structures are in place, language does not have to carry the entire weight of explanation. People can see where they fit.

This is already informing how I structure keyword systems, platform strategies, and content distribution for client work. Grounded in how language behaves across systems rather than how we expect it to. If you are trying to understand how your language travels, where it starts to lose connection, or how to design for participation from the start, this is exactly what The Algorithm Witch does.

title: Posting Less Won’t Save You
excerpt: We have been blessed by the social media data gods this past month. 🙏🏽
body_html:
We have been blessed by the social media data gods this past month. 🙏🏽

Yes, that’s right, Sprout Social, Buffer, and HubSpot all dropped their 2026 reports and the LinkedIn Engineering blog released a new feed analysis. Across the data, one claim stood out: posting less will help growth.

It sounds tidy. It also collapses several different trends into one convenient myth.

New cross-platform engagement data suggests something more interesting is occurring. Engagement did fall on some platforms in the past year, especially Instagram and Threads. But it also grew on others like Facebook and Pinterest, and held relatively stable on TikTok. Meanwhile LinkedIn still maintains some of the strongest engagement rates in the dataset.

The real story is that feeds became denser, AI made content production dramatically cheaper, and the metrics marketers rely on no longer capture what platforms are actually optimizing for. In other words, content supply rose faster than audience attention.

Posting less does not actively help your growth. Posting less may protect your average per-post vanity metrics, but there is no dispute that not posting will hurt you. The tradeoff is aggregate growth versus per-post efficiency. I’ll explain how the data shows a fragmentation in engagement driving platform inconsistencies and unpredictability and explain why the answer is adding value with consistency and format experimentation that you have capacity for.

01What the cross-platform data reveals

Buffer’s 2026 analysis examined more than 52 million posts across major platforms, tracking changes in engagement rate and content performance between 2024 and 2025. These shifts moved in opposite directions across platforms. If engagement were collapsing across the board, we would expect synchronized declines.

The platforms that rely heavily on relationship networks and conversational engagement held relatively steady. Platforms dominated by short-form video discovery feeds experienced sharper engagement-rate declines.

That difference implies engagement rate measures interaction relative to impressions, not reach itself. When a platform dramatically expands distribution, especially through algorithmic discovery, engagement rate can fall even if total views and audience size increase.

02The “post less to grow more” myth

Each platform’s interaction patterns produce different baselines. A 2% rate on X might be strong. A 2% rate on LinkedIn would be failure.

Buffer’s frequency analysis complicates this. Accounts that did not post in a given week consistently underperformed their own average growth baseline. Even posting one to two times per week improved growth relative to silence. Researchers described this pattern as a “no-post penalty.”

However, they also found something that explains why the “post less” narrative persists. As posting frequency increases, reach per individual post often declines. Posting more can increase total audience growth while simultaneously lowering the apparent efficiency of each individual post. So marketers looking only at per-post engagement metrics might conclude they should post less. But if they zoom out to total reach and follower growth, the opposite may be true. So when looking at what metrics should drive your strategy, you should consider your own growth and efficiency goals. For example, you might find link clicks are more valuable than impressions and that some platforms don’t drive clicks. That can help you identify which platforms are worth your energy, which are worth the commitment to drive growth, and which ones aren’t efficient.

Frequency is part of the story, but across six platforms, Buffer also found that posts with replies present consistently outperformed those without, with the largest lifts on Threads and LinkedIn. Replies are one of the few cross-platform behaviors Buffer found consistently associated with stronger engagement. Conversation signals are becoming one of the most consistent ranking inputs.

LinkedIn’s Engineering blog points to deeper shift in how the feed operates. Ranking no longer relies only on actions like clicks, reactions, comments, and re-shares, or even dwell time. The system now interprets what a post is about and how it aligns with a user’s evolving interests, using those patterns to decide what should be seen next. That means even on a platform with relatively strong engagement rates, surface interaction counts capture only a small part of how distribution actually works.

Fragmentation is also visible in audience behavior. Sprout’s 2026 data shows a widening gap between where audiences plan to spend more time, led by Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube, and where brands plan to pour more effort, led by TikTok and Instagram.

03The AI supply shock

There is another structural change influencing engagement: AI.

Given that 80% of teams now produce AI-assisted content, the feed’s content supply has likely grown dramatically without an increase in audience attention. As production costs fall, the volume of content competing for attention rises. The feed becomes denser even if audience demand stays the same and the result is a classic supply shock. Content volume rises faster than attention capacity. The average quality of posts decreases while the number of posts increases. In this environment, engagement rates fall even if audience demand remains stable. Platforms respond by leaning more heavily on algorithmic filtering, predictive ranking, and recommendation systems. All of this has created a landscape where distribution becomes less predictable and differentiation becomes more valuable.

AI-assisted content production has increased the volume of visually similar posts in the system, which raises the importance of consistent positioning and recognizable framing for classification models. These structural decisions become necessary when you look at how individual platforms behave. Each network distributes content through a different ranking system, which produces very different engagement patterns. The only way to gain traction is to be accessible and available to classification.

04Platform analysis

This is exactly why I built the Platform Stack Quiz. To find which platforms fits your team, your content capacity, your audience, and the way your organization already communicates. The breakdown below is meant to help you think like a strategist building a stack that can hold.

Instagram

Instagram experienced the largest engagement-rate drop in the dataset. Buffer notes that the platform has increasingly pushed creators toward views as a primary success metric, which makes engagement rate a less complete measure than it used to be.

Previously, Instagram distribution was more tightly anchored to the social graph. Meta reported that Reels now account for more than 50% of time spent on Instagram. So when engagement drops on Instagram, that it may simply mean the metric marketers being used are outdated. Video reach expanded dramatically, which increases impressions faster than interactions. Meanwhile Buffer’s data shows that carousel posts still generate the highest engagement rates, while Reels produce the most reach.

That pattern suggests Instagram now operates through two parallel systems: Reels function as discovery infrastructure, while carousel posts create depth through saves, swipes, and longer viewing time. Engagement-rate declines may reflect the shift toward discovery content.

For climate, clean energy, and advocacy teams, the practical application of Instagram should be treated like a two-layer communication system. Reels help organizations reach new audiences and introduce issues to people who may not already follow them. Carousels and explainers then convert that attention into understanding through save-worthy information and deeper context. Organizations that perform well on Instagram tend to treat the platform as an extension of their knowledge base.

TikTok

TikTok engagement remained largely stable year over year with Buffer data showing engagement shifting only +3% year over year. The platform rewards high posting volume and rapid experimentation because viral distribution creates asymmetric upside. Large-scale analyses of social platform performance show TikTok engagement rates changing only modestly relative to other networks, even as overall content volume increased across platforms.

Unlike Instagram, TikTok does not rely primarily on a follower graph for distribution. Content is tested through recommendation cycles in the For You feed, which makes the platform behave more like a continuous experimentation environment than a traditional social network. TikTok’s own documentation describes the For You system as ranking videos using signals such as watch time, completion rate, likes, comments, shares, and replays to determine whether a video should be distributed to broader audiences.

Posting less on TikTok usually means running fewer experiments, which lowers the probability of breakout performance. Because videos are distributed in staged recommendation waves, early retention and engagement signals determine whether the system expands reach to progressively larger audiences.

For communications teams, this structure favors organizations that treat TikTok as a learning environment. Short, conversational videos explaining a policy change, reacting to news in your field, or showing staff discussing their work often outperform highly produced campaign videos because they generate stronger watch time and comments. Consistent posting increases the number of experiments the system can run on your content, raising the likelihood that one piece will break beyond your existing audience and introduce new supporters to the organization.

LinkedIn

LinkedIn experienced a modest engagement decline but still maintains one of the highest median engagement rates in Buffer’s dataset. The strongest format remains document carousels, which consistently generate higher interaction than most other post types.

LinkedIn’s Engineering blog helps explain why. The platform’s feed ranking system has shifted toward a model that understands what a post is actually about and how it connects to a user’s evolving professional interests. Instead of relying only on network proximity or early engagement, the system uses large language models to classify content semantically and match it to patterns in what people have been reading, engaging with, and returning to over time. The system looks at sequences of engagement to predict what someone is likely to find useful next, which means content that aligns with a user’s current line of thinking is more likely to travel beyond an author’s immediate network.

Conversation still plays an outsized role in that process. Buffer’s analysis shows posts that receive replies see roughly 30% higher engagement, and profiles that regularly respond to comments outperform those that do not. LinkedIn Engineering similarly describes the feed as a relevance-ranking system designed to surface professional conversations users are likely to participate in, differentiating that from content they might passively consume.

For climate organizations, the accounts that perform best tend to publish ideas that invite discussion. Staff experts sharing perspective on policy changes, program outcomes, or field research outperform brand posts because the system recognizes the author’s topical authority. In practice, this makes LinkedIn more of a public forum where organizations build credibility through participation.

Threads

Threads saw one of the sharper engagement declines in the dataset. At the same time, it still shows the largest engagement lift from replies, roughly 42%. That pattern suggests the system continues to reward conversation depth rather than passive consumption.

That structure aligns with how Meta has been positioning Threads as a public discussion space. Distribution increasingly blends interest modeling with conversation signals, which means posts that generate sustained replies continue traveling further through the network.

For organizational communications, this creates an opportunity for perspective and commentary. Posts that frame a policy update, respond to breaking news in your field, or ask a clear question often generate stronger reply chains than announcement-style posts. In practice this means the platform behaves like a hybrid between Twitter and Reddit, where thoughtful participation compounds visibility over time.

Facebook

Despite the cultural narrative that the platform is fading, Facebook is surprisingly one of the most stable environments for brand interaction.

Facebook’s feed blends social graph ranking with interest modeling, which means content from organizations people already follow continues to receive consistent distribution. In contrast to discovery-heavy platforms, engagement on Facebook tends to accumulate through existing relationships and repeat interactions more reliably.

Consumer research also shows Facebook remains one of the primary places users expect to interact with brands for customer support and information. That expectation keeps organizational pages relevant even as newer platforms attract more cultural attention.

For climate, clean energy, and advocacy organizations this stability can be significant. Facebook often performs best when it functions as a community layer. Updates about programs, local events, volunteer opportunities, or policy changes tend to perform well because audiences already have an existing relationship with the organization. The platform may not be sexy, but it continues to operate as a durable infrastructure for maintaining public connection.

Pinterest

Pinterest operates as a hybrid between search and social discovery, which makes it less vulnerable to the engagement volatility affecting purely feed-based networks. Unlike traditional social feeds, Pinterest really functions like a visual search engine.

The Pinterest feed is indexed around intent signals such as keywords, image recognition, and user saving behavior. That structure allows posts to continue circulating long after publication rather than disappearing quickly in a chronological or algorithmic feed.

Video has become more prominent on the platform, but static images and instructional graphics still perform strongly because they align with how users search and save content. Engagement on Pinterest accumulates through saves and rediscovery rather than rapid comment cycles.

For nonprofit organizations this creates a different kind of opportunity. Educational graphics, explainers, resource guides, and visual summaries of research can continue attracting traffic months after they are published. In practice, Pinterest behaves like a long-tail discovery layer where useful information compounds over time.

Reddit

Reddit operates differently from most social platforms because content distribution is governed primarily by community voting and moderation structures. Visibility depends on how users upvote, comment on, and share posts within individual subreddits, which makes Reddit function like public forum infrastructure.

This structure shapes how organizations should approach the platform. Consumer research from Sprout Social suggests Reddit tends to work better as a listening environment instead of a traditional publishing channel. Communities are highly sensitive to promotional behavior, and posts that read like scheduled marketing content are often ignored or removed by moderators.

Organizations that succeed on Reddit usually participate as contributors: answering questions, sharing expertise when relevant to a discussion, and engaging within established communities. Providing string-free expertise on Reddit can generate more credibility than pushing links or campaign announcements. For advocacy teams, the platform can be valuable for understanding public sentiment around policy issues, identifying emerging narratives, and participating in conversations where genuine expertise is welcomed.

Twitter

Twitter (X) recorded one of the largest engagement increases in the dataset, but that number requires caution. The platform’s monetization and verification changes significantly altered distribution dynamics over the past year.

Buffer’s analysis suggests engagement among non-premium accounts has dropped sharply in some segments, with median engagement approaching zero in certain months. That pattern indicates engagement growth may be concentrated among a smaller set of accounts that receive algorithmic prioritization.

The shift is likely connected to changes in the platform’s subscription and verification systems, which influence visibility in replies, recommendations, and conversation ranking. As a result, the engagement environment on X may be structurally distorted relative to earlier periods when distribution was less tied to account status.

For nonprofit organizations this creates a volatile strategic landscape. Accounts that do not participate in the platform’s paid features may never see their posts travel. X could function as a real-time commentary space during major news cycles, but relying on it as a consistent reach channel has become significantly less predictable without buying into the platform.

05What this means for strategy

Taken together, these datasets point to a structural change in how social distribution works.

Engagement is diverging and each platform’s distribution model now produces its own engagement pattern. Platforms built around conversational networks and relationship graphs have remained relatively stable, while platforms centered on algorithmic discovery have seen sharper engagement-rate declines as reach expands faster than interaction.

Posting less does not appear to improve growth. Across millions of accounts, Buffer’s analysis consistently shows that silence consistently underperforms even modest publishing activity. Accounts that stop posting temporarily protect their per-post engagement averages, but they also disappear from recommendation cycles, conversation threads, and follower feeds. Growth slows because the system has fewer opportunities to evaluate and distribute new content.

AI has also revolutionized the economics of content production. HubSpot’s research shows that most marketing teams now use AI in their workflows and that a majority believe it represents the largest disruption to marketing in decades. The cost of producing content has dropped dramatically, but the supply of attention has not increased at the same rate. In practice that means the feed has become denser while audience attention remains fixed. ￼

Those structural changes explain the patterns visible across platforms. Instagram expanded discovery through Reels, which increased impressions faster than interactions. TikTok continues to function as a large-scale experimentation environment where breakout distribution depends on retention signals. LinkedIn and Threads reward conversation depth through replies and discussion threads. Pinterest behaves more like search infrastructure where useful content compounds over time. Facebook remains anchored in relationship networks that sustain stable engagement among existing communities.

When these systems are compared side by side, the idea that “posting less improves quality” becomes harder to support. The platforms still reward consistency and experimentation and certainly favor volume, within a clear editorial frame, above silence.

The strategic question you have to consider is how to produce enough signal for the system to understand what you are about.

06The feed got louder

The modern feed processes a far larger volume of content than it did only a few years ago. Much of that content is now produced with AI assistance, which means posts can be generated faster and at greater scale than before.

Platforms respond to that density by leaning more heavily on prediction systems. Ranking models evaluate not only likes and comments but also dwell time, completion rate, conversation depth, and behavioral patterns that signal genuine interest. LinkedIn’s Engineering blog, for example, describes ranking models that incorporate multiple engagement signals alongside semantic relevance and expertise classification.

The result is a distribution environment where visibility depends on consistent patterns. Algorithms need repeated signals to understand what an account represents, who its audience is, and which users are likely to find its content meaningful.

For organizations working in climate, policy, and advocacy communications, a steady cadence of publishing helps classification systems identify topical authority and audience alignment. Conversation signals such as replies and discussion threads increasingly influence how far posts travel. Platforms built around discovery reward experimentation, while platforms built around relationships reward participation. When content volume increases, differentiation becomes more valuable.

AI lowered the cost of production but did not lower the cost of taste. The challenge now is not simply to produce more content. It is to publish consistently enough for the system to recognize your signal while developing a point of view strong enough to cut through the noise. In the AI era, clarity is becoming one of the scarcest resources in the feed.

intro_closing: I’ll explain how the data shows a fragmentation in engagement driving platform inconsistencies and unpredictability and explain why the answer is adding value with consistency and format experimentation that you have capacity for.

title: Born to Scroll
excerpt: Clicking was the standard way to navigate the web. Scrolling was considered bad or risky design. Early usability research warned about the “below the fold” problem,…
body_html:
🎶 Born to scroll the endless feed 🎶

Clicking was the standard way to navigate the web. Scrolling was considered bad or risky design. Early usability research warned about the “below the fold” problem, because users historically paid much more attention to content at the top of a page. Jakob Nielsen and other usability researchers emphasized placing important information immediately on page load, since early studies showed around 80% of viewing time happened above the fold. That guidance reflected how websites functioned at the time. Pages were living documents where users scanned, evaluated links, and clicked to decide where to go next, like turning pages in an encyclopedia.

Modern research shows that behavior has evolved. In Nielsen Norman Group’s 2018 study, 57% of viewing time still occurred above the fold, but attention below the fold had increased significantly. Roughly 43% of viewing time now happens further down the page, meaning users are interacting far more with content that requires scrolling than they did in earlier studies.

Enter: the feed. Since 2018, platforms like TikTok, Instagram, and Twitter trained billions of people to navigate information through continuous vertical motion. Short-form video accelerated this dramatically. TikTok in particular turned scrolling into a continuous discovery mechanism. Media scholar Tarleton Gillespie (2018) argues that platforms do not simply host content but actively organize and shape how participation unfolds online, structuring visibility and public discourse through their systems. We lost interest in turning the page and we learned to explore interfaces by scrolling. The gesture became the default way people move through digital space.

Today the long scroll page has become an increasingly common website structure on the internet. Organizations can present their story through a single vertically structured surface that guides visitors through the core narrative as they move down the page. From my perspective building sites and studying how people actually navigate them, the most effective approach is a hybrid structure: treat the homepage as a guided scroll, then connect it to deeper pages where people can explore programs, research, or ways to participate. Usability research still shows that attention drops after the first screenful, which is why the most important ideas should appear early in the page while deeper content lives behind clear navigation for visitors who want to go further.

Collections like One Page Love curate thousands of examples of websites where an entire digital presence lives inside a single narrative scroll. Browsing through them can be useful for understanding how sections are sequenced, where calls to action appear, and how organizations guide attention without relying on complex navigation.

For nonprofit organizations considering a website redesign, the long scroll can be a practical structure that reflects how people already move through information online.

01Feed.com

Is a modern homepage just another social timeline? I think yes.

I see more organizations blurring the boundary between their website and their social presence. A TikTok profile can function like a miniature media hub. A communications director’s LinkedIn page can become a living archive of an organization’s expertise. In certain contexts these spaces behave like or even replace websites themselves.

There are still important differences. Ownership, indexing, and long-term stability remain fundamentally stronger on the open web. I wrote about those platform dynamics in my previous article on how different social systems rank and distribute content, where I explain why each platform incentivizes different kinds of behavior.

At the interface level the logic of websites and social platforms is converging. Scrolling has become the common language through which we navigate both.

02Design for the scroll

When I build websites now, I often start from a simple structure.

Instead of forcing users to navigate through a complicated menu, the homepage becomes a guided journey. Key ideas appear in sequence. Each section introduces another layer of context. If someone wants deeper detail they can branch into the underlying pages.

The goal is to integrate structure into the scrolling experience. In practice this means designing your homepage the same way platforms design feeds. Each section should carry a clear idea. Visual rhythm means information density needs variation so attention does not collapse halfway down the page. Assume your website visitors will scroll and make that behavior the interface.

03Feed within a feed

If I haven’t convinced you yet, let me convincingly deploy some inception: most people arrive at websites through feeds. Social media is a top news source for most Americans. That means we are primed, ready, and already scrolling from our phones. It’s likely that the most intentional users of your site will be visitors acquire from platforms like TikTok. You need to stop the scroll and move them from that platform to yours. They click from Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok or follow a link from a newsletter. The entry point is already part of a scrolling environment. A page that continues that motion feels frictionless.

This has created new variations of the traditional marketing funnel. Organizations now maintain multiple entry points connected to different feeds. A TikTok audience may land on a simplified landing page designed for fast comprehension. A LinkedIn audience may arrive through a research article or long-form analysis. A newsletter reader may enter through a deep blog post.

The website becomes the center that connects those streams. Each feed delivers attention in a slightly different way. Your site absorbs that attention and gives it structure.

04The scroll changed everything

Scrolling is now the default motion of the internet. Feeds changed the way people move through information. Your website needs to adapt. Marketing is now fundamentally multi-channel. HubSpot (2026) reports that 75% of teams operate across five or more platforms, while social media, websites, and search remain the highest-performing channels by ROI. In practice, this means organizations no longer operate a single digital presence but an ecosystem of interconnected surfaces.

The long-scroll page is an architectural response to the behavioral patterns platforms trained us to follow. In other words, the web learned how to scroll. Designing with that reality in mind means arranging information so it unfolds naturally inside the gesture people already use to explore digital space.
