title: The Recommendation Engine Learned Where You Live
excerpt: You may have noticed something over the past few years. Creators in your city appear more often. Small businesses near you surface without you searching for them.…
body_html:
You may have noticed something over the past few years. Creators in your city appear more often. Small businesses near you surface without you searching for them. Community events, neighborhood commentary, and regional creators circulate more heavily inside your recommendations. Geographic relevance is becoming part of the architecture of distribution, layered on top of interest graphs as a probability engine that helps platforms anchor attention in physical proximity.

Location is becoming a stronger signal inside ranking systems. Feeds are learning where you are.

As platforms mature and markets (over)saturate, geographic relevance becomes valuable infrastructure. It improves ad targeting, increases real-world conversion, and strengthens retention by anchoring content in physical proximity.

Before fully location-weighted feeds emerged, platforms (TikTok, Meta) quietly expanded geo-based targeting in ads. Now we are seeing stronger local signals in organic distribution as well. If you live in Austin, you are more likely to see Austin creators than someone living in New York. Interest graphs are being layered with geographic probability.

01Location Discovery Is Officially Here

In early 2026, TikTok rolled out a new Local Feed in the U.S., a dedicated discovery surface that curates content from creators, small businesses, and events near your physical location. The feature appears as a tab on the TikTok home screen and surfaces posts based on your location, topic relevance, and recency. Users must opt in to share precise GPS location data for that feed, and it is off by default, but once enabled it shows organic nearby content separate from the traditional For You recommendations. A video tagged with a local place or referencing community events can be shown to nearby users through the Local Feed, creating a new organic surface for visibility and potential conversion without paid ads, especially valuable for physical businesses and nonprofit outreach.

This is the first major rollout of a location-based content surface in the U.S. since TikTok unveiled “Nearby Feeds” in Europe, where posts are shown based on location, topic, and posting time.

Instagram has also introduced earlier features that lean into locality and user-selected personalization, including optional location sharing and tools that let users influence what shows up in their Reels feed, signaling a broader industry trend toward local and personalized discovery systems.

Take the Quiz

02How Each Platform Actually Works

Location signals have added to behavioral ranking. Geographic proximity now operates alongside watch time, engagement velocity, semantic clarity, and predicted interest. The feed is still governed by probabilistic modeling. What has changed is the number of contextual layers influencing that model.

To understand how location interacts with distribution, we need to look at how each platform actually ranks content.

03TikTok

Retention and Testing

TikTok’s For You feed is driven by a recommendation system that predicts user interest based on interactions, video information, and watch behavior. According to TikTok’s own documentation, ranking considers metrics such as watch time, completion rate, replays, likes, comments, and shares. The system evaluates how long viewers stay and whether they engage, then uses those signals to determine whether a video should be shown to broader audiences.

Videos are distributed in waves. Early performance data informs whether content is tested with larger groups. If retention remains strong and interaction compounds, distribution expands.

Hashtags can clarify context, but TikTok confirms they are only one of several signals under “video information.” Retention and engagement carry greater structural weight in determining scale.

In January 2026, TikTok’s U.S. operations formally transitioned into a new entity, TikTok USDS Joint Venture LLC. The joint venture has stated it will retrain, test, and update the content recommendation algorithm on U.S. user data. While TikTok continues to describe the core signals that shape ranking, the precise structure and weighting of the algorithm remain proprietary and may evolve under this new governance model.

TikTok indexes caption text, on-screen text, and spoken audio through automated transcription. Clear keyword usage across these layers strengthens semantic classification more effectively than stacking trending hashtags without topical coherence. TikTok is optimized for velocity and attention density. Content that sustains watch time relative to length is more likely to move beyond its initial audience.

TikTok is a strong fit for nonprofit teams that can translate their work into culturally fluent short-form storytelling, whether that means staff who understand how to adapt trends like Southern Alliance for Clean Energy’s “when the Gen Z social team writes the script,” team members who are can chill out and “yap” directly to camera, or internal creators willing to grow into full “Staples baddie” energy and carry the voice of the organization consistently on-screen.

04Instagram

Interest Modeling + Social Graph

Instagram blends interest-based recommendation with social proximity. Feed and Stories stay relationship-weighted, so accounts you regularly interact with keep getting priority, while Reels and Explore lean harder on predicted interest and recommended distribution. Instagram’s own guidance describes ranking as a set of prediction systems that estimate which posts you are likely to spend time on and engage with, including signals such as time spent, likes, comments, shares, and other actions. Internal Meta engineering documentation has repeatedly identified “time spent” and “meaningful interactions” as core ranking inputs across surfaces.

Reels distribution is especially sensitive to watch behavior and sharing. Mosseri has described sharing as a central outcome Instagram optimizes for, and reporting on his comments highlights “sends” as a high-value signal for Reels ranking and reach beyond followers. Reels compete through watch time and saves.

Stories reinforce relationship scoring through the actions that look like real connection, including replies, DMs, and repeated profile behavior. Instagram’s public materials repeatedly frame ranking as prediction around the actions people actually take, and private sharing fits that logic because it signals strong intent and relationship depth.

Carousel posts extend dwell time through swipe behavior, which increases time spent per post and often correlates with higher save rates.

Stories reinforce relationship signals. Direct messages, replies, profile taps, and sticker interactions feed proximity scoring that influences future visibility in Feed and Stories placement.

Direct messaging has become increasingly central to growth mechanics. Instagram’s prompts encouraging users to send a message after following and the platform’s broader emphasis on private sharing reflect a prioritization of one-to-one interaction. DMs, shares to friends, story replies, and profile taps feed proximity modeling. When users message or privately share content, the system interprets that as a strong relationship signal, increasing the likelihood that future posts from that account surface in Feed and Stories. Many creators and brands are now layering automation tools like ManyChat into this dynamic, triggering keyword-based auto-responses in DMs to deliver resources, links, or follow-ups at scale. For nonprofits, this is worth considering carefully: automated direct response systems can help capture interest and distribute information quickly, but they must be aligned with voice, trust, and capacity to respond thoughtfully in an environment where private interaction increasingly shapes visibility.

Hashtags can contribute categorization. Meta has stated that hashtags help the system understand context, but they are not a primary distribution lever. Ranking weight sits in predicted engagement, saves, shares, profile actions, and behavioral consistency across posts.

Aesthetic coherence is central. Instagram’s computer vision systems analyze visual patterns, and repeated topic signals across posts strengthen classification confidence. Consistent subject matter and recognizable framing increase the probability that content is shown to similar audiences again.

Instagram rewards recognizable positioning. Nonprofits that perform well on Instagram tend to share a few structural characteristics. They curate a clean and consistent visual language. Their website positioning is clear enough that captions can echo it without confusion. Their SEO foundation produces stable keyword architecture that translates naturally into posts and Reels. They maintain a strong internal database of language, research, and resources, allowing them to repurpose credible information into save-worthy carousels and shareable explainer videos. When brand clarity, research fluency, and visual coherence align, Instagram becomes an extension of existing authority rather than a separate performance channel.

Threads

Threads uses a recommendation model that blends interest-based ranking with Instagram graph proximity, while placing heavier emphasis on conversation and reply depth. Meta has described Threads ranking as prioritizing posts likely to generate replies, engagement, and time spent, with Instagram network signals informing early distribution. In February 2026, Meta introduced a feature called “Dear Algo,” allowing users to explicitly request more or less of certain topics in their feed. The adjustment temporarily influences recommendation weighting, signaling a shift toward more visible, participatory personalization layered on top of engagement modeling. Compared to Instagram, Threads favors text density, conversational velocity, and topical clustering more than visual coherence.

Threads works best for organizations that already produce strong long-form content and consistent Instagram output. If your team has excess capacity and a clear voice, Threads can function as a conversational extension, directing audiences toward blog posts, research, webinars, and Reels. It rewards organizations that can sustain dialogue, respond quickly, and participate in ongoing topic threads rather than simply broadcast announcements.

05LinkedIn

Engagement Probability + Dwell Time

LinkedIn’s engineering team has publicly described a feed ranking system built on predicted engagement probability and network effects. Models estimate the likelihood of clicks, reactions, comments, and shares, along with expected downstream amplification and upstream value to the author. In 2020, LinkedIn detailed how dwell time and “skip probability” were incorporated into feed ranking to reduce low-interest posts and surface content members actually spend time with.

Early comment activity influences expansion beyond first-degree connections. When interaction compounds quickly, posts are more likely to be introduced to broader networks based on predicted relevance.

In November 2025, LinkedIn engineers clarified that Feed visibility relies on behavioral signals derived from member activity, network proximity, and engagement patterns. The same update acknowledged that content creation volume on LinkedIn has grown rapidly over the past year, increasing competition for attention.

Hashtags help connect posts to topics. Distribution itself is governed by engagement probability, dwell time modeling, and network effects.

LinkedIn rewards value density and conversation. Posts that prompt sustained reading and meaningful comments generate stronger dwell signals and are more likely to circulate beyond immediate connections.

Independent analysis from Shield Index tracking approximately 50,000 LinkedIn posts shows that breakout behavior varies significantly by account size. Smaller accounts can experience reach multiples many times larger than their follower base on top-performing posts, while larger accounts tend to see narrower variation between typical and breakout posts.

LinkedIn is structurally advantaged for industry long-haulers. Professionals who have been in their field for a decade or more, founders, executive directors, policy staff, and small business contractors often perform best because the platform weights credibility signals embedded in profile data, network proximity, and industry relevance. When someone with established tenure posts, the system already has rich behavioral context about who might care.

This is also the platform where life events amplify reach. LinkedIn explicitly surfaces milestones such as new roles, launches, promotions, layoffs, and funding announcements. Public reporting has documented how posts tied to job transitions and layoffs frequently generate unusually high engagement, driven by network notification mechanics and heightened social relevance. The platform notifies connections of role changes and highlights milestones in-feed, creating built-in distribution advantages for posts attached to those moments. For nonprofit leaders, this is the key. Executive transitions, major grant announcements, organizational launches, coalition formations, and campaign milestones align naturally with LinkedIn’s amplification structure. When those moments are paired with authentic internal perspectives, engagement probability increases because the post invites conversation and community. In this environment, fluency often outperforms trend adaptation.

06YouTube

Search + Session Duration

YouTube operates simultaneously as a recommendation and a search engine. Videos surface through homepage recommendations, suggested video modeling, and search indexing. Owned by Google, YouTube also benefits from deep integration with the broader search ecosystem, where videos frequently appear in Google results, featured snippets, and video carousels. Discovery and authority are layered rather than separate systems, with performance inside YouTube reinforcing visibility across Google’s search infrastructure.

YouTube Shorts now generate more than 200 billion views per day globally, serving as a high-velocity discovery layer. Shorts introduce viewers to channels, topics, and creators. However, durable authority is still driven by long-form watch time, session duration, transcript language, and channel consistency. YouTube’s ranking systems prioritize viewer satisfaction signals, including average view duration, relative retention, and the likelihood that a video contributes to longer session time on the platform.

Titles, descriptions, chapters, captions, and spoken words are indexed. YouTube’s automatic transcription system feeds directly into search visibility. Clear topic alignment across video metadata and on-screen language strengthens classification confidence. Retention compounds visibility; videos that sustain watch time relative to length are more likely to be recommended again.

YouTube is structurally advantageous for nonprofit teams that already produce long-form content. Webinars, research briefings, conference panels, town halls, educational series, and recorded events translate naturally into long-form video libraries. Organizations that maintain consistent programming schedules and topic architecture can build search authority over time.

Shorts provide a bridge. Clips extracted from webinars, keynote moments, research highlights, or policy explainers can function as entry points that route viewers into longer videos. When short-form discovery is paired with structured long-form libraries, the channel compounds visibility rather than chasing isolated viral moments. Channels that treat YouTube as a knowledge infrastructure tend to build sustainable reach.

07Bluesky

Protocol-Driven Discovery

Bluesky operates differently from centralized ranking platforms. Built on the AT Protocol, it allows users to select or create custom feeds rather than relying on a single opaque recommendation system. Feeds can be organized around topics, communities, moderation preferences, or curated lists, shifting discovery toward feed selection and network clustering rather than engagement prediction.

Visibility depends on which feeds include your content and how your account is integrated into community graphs. Engagement is still important, but distribution is less driven by velocity modeling and more influenced by participation within defined discourse networks.

For nonprofits, Bluesky can function as a relationship platform. Organizations that need to be connected with reporters, policy analysts, researchers, and niche industry communities may find it structurally useful. Journalists and media workers have adopted Bluesky in significant numbers following changes to X, and topic-specific feeds allow nonprofits to surface directly inside professional conversations. Bluesky rewards clarity of expertise, consistent participation, and network presence.

08Twitter

Monetization on X has fundamentally changed distribution. Accounts with paid blue checkmarks receive algorithmic prioritization in replies and visibility, creating a pay-for-amplification dynamic that has altered how timelines feel and function.

Reporting has shown growth among paid accounts while many legacy institutional voices saw volatility in reach, and user experiments have documented instances where Elon Musk’s own account appeared with disproportionate frequency in feeds. The platform’s revenue model increasingly ties visibility to subscription and creator payouts, blending engagement ranking with financial incentives.

I cannot recommend that nonprofits or mission driven brands treat X as a primary channel.

09The Strategy Incentives

Retention TikTok is engineered around retention curves and attention density. Distribution expands when watch time compounds and early interaction signals hold. Velocity determines testing. Testing determines scale.

Relationship Instagram organizes visibility through relationship modeling layered with predicted engagement. Proximity, private interaction, aesthetic coherence, and behavioral consistency reinforce classification confidence.

Authority LinkedIn ranks through engagement probability, dwell time, and network effects. Professional proximity and comment depth influence expansion beyond first-degree connections.

Search YouTube compounds authority through session duration and search indexing. Long-form watch time, transcript clarity, and channel consistency accumulate over time inside Google’s broader search infrastructure.

Community Bluesky distributes through feed choice and community clustering. Inclusion inside the right feed ecosystems determines visibility more than velocity modeling.

Choosing a platform means choosing the behavioral logic you are willing to operate inside, the signals you are prepared to produce consistently, and the type of institutional strength you want to build. The question is which incentive structure aligns with how your organization actually operates.

10📍You Are Here

Location feeds move discovery logic towards place-based relevance. As location becomes a stronger ranking variable, organizations should consider where their work is meant to land.

  • Are you based in a city, state, or region?

  • Are you trying to scale nationally or internationally?

  • Does your work depend on geographic trust?

  • Are your audiences physically clustered?

Local density can amplify, or limit, visibility. A nonprofit operating in Chicago may see stronger exposure among Chicago-based users as proximity weighting increases. Expansion beyond that region requires intentional effort rather than accidental spillover.

As geography becomes embedded in ranking systems, content can surface based on where someone is at the moment of discovery, not only what they have previously engaged with.

Every algorithmic layer that incorporates location means your content may be surfaced based on where people are not just what they historically engage with, but where they’re physically situated at the moment of discovery. For mission-driven organizations, location now influences how efficiently content translates into turnout, attendance, membership, or conversion.

11Privacy, Power, and Control

Location-based ranking introduces a new layer of convenience and a new layer of vulnerability. It is reasonable to question what it means to share increasingly precise geographic data with technology companies whose governance structures continue to shift, whose moderation systems are partially automated, and whose cooperation with governments varies by jurisdiction.

Opt-in systems are framed as optional. But when location becomes structurally advantageous for distribution, a quiet pressure can emerge. Does declining to share location reduce visibility? Does withholding proximity signals create subtle ranking friction? Platforms do not explicitly state penalties, but incentive structures shape behavior over time.

If digital feeds begin continue to mirror physical reality then online environments will inevitably continue to replicate and perpetuate offline patterns of segregation. Cities are stratified by race, income, and access. If geographic clustering becomes a dominant discovery layer, digital visibility may increasingly reflect those same divides. As we see in physical environments, location can be leveraged to tighten the loop between community and conversion or narrow the radius of exposure.

For organizations, the question is how much proximity you are willing to trade for reach, and what kind of digital public sphere you want to participate in.

12Invest in Your Website Today

This evolution has two strategic implications:

  1. Audience Proximity: Content designed for a regional audience may perform better in localized surfaces than generic global content, even if overall format and retention signals are strong.

  2. AI, SEO, and Social SEO: Location signals strengthen when your content also reinforces keyword relevance tied to places (e.g., “Chicago nonprofit volunteer opportunities,” “Brooklyn community event”). These terms then feed both web search and social search logic, increasing discoverability everywhere

Social platforms interpret you, but your website belongs to you.

On social, reach is probabilistic. You can optimize for retention, engagement velocity, and semantic clarity. You can test formats and measure performance. Even with expertise, prediction is imperfect as ranking systems update and recalibrate.

On the web, you control structure. Search engines index full pages. You determine title architecture, define keywords, and decide internal linking. Website build topic continuity over time.

Long-form blogs and YouTube deepen semantic authority. Keywords can be carried consistently across pages, transcripts, captions, and newsletters. That continuity strengthens discoverability across systems.

Web and long-form infrastructure compounds, while social reach will always fluctuate. Organizations that anchor their strategy in owned platforms while using social for distribution reduce volatility and increase durability.

13Focus on What Lasts

Choose your terms carefully and carry them across your website, your videos, your captions, your newsletters. Let your vocabulary become a spine that runs through everything you publish. Let it mean something each time it appears.

Measure retention and watch where attention drops. Authority accumulates from coherence sustained across surfaces and coherence compounds most reliably where you own the structure, the archive, and the terms by which you are discovered.

title: Is the Short-Form Video Market Oversaturated?
excerpt: What began on a single app that penetrated culture so deeply the format borrowed its name now anchors digital communication across nearly every major platform. Short-form…
body_html:
What began on a single app that penetrated culture so deeply the format borrowed its name now anchors digital communication across nearly every major platform. Short-form video carries professional commentary on LinkedIn, organizing strategy on TikTok, brand storytelling on Instagram, and podcast distribution on YouTube Shorts within the same vertical frame.

In 2025, short-form video continued its rapid expansion. According to the Metricool 2025 State of Short-Form Video Report, short-form publishing volume increased by roughly 71 percent year over year, while the number of accounts using the format rose approximately 51 percent, with TikTok video output growing by more than 150 percent. In 2024, YouTube Shorts averaged around 70 billion daily views, reflecting its scale as a distribution layer for vertical video. LinkedIn reported that video uploads increased by more than 20 percent, with views rising 34 percent year over year since 2024. Meta stated that Reels across Instagram and Facebook operate at roughly a $50 billion annual revenue run rate, underscoring the business centrality of short-form video by the end of 2025. Industry estimates place TikTok’s annual advertising revenue above $30 billion, reinforcing its continued economic weight in the ecosystem.

Participation has scaled at extraordinary speed. At the same time, something more subtle has shifted inside the content itself.

The End of the Aspirational Influencer Era

For much of the 2010s, short-form content revolved around aspiration. Influencer culture centered on lifestyle aesthetics, curated consumption, and performative success. Brand partnerships mirrored that tone. Glossy travel, luxury routines, product unboxings, and highly stylized personal branding dominated feed visibility. Audience behavior has recalibrated what influence means in 2026.

Trust surveys over the last three years show where authority is consolidating. Edelman’s 2026 Trust Barometer reports that scientists (76%) and teachers (73%) rank among the most trusted voices globally. This reaffirms a trend visible across digital platforms since 2023, Morning Consult’s research found that while trust in influencers among younger audiences increased, the traits respondents valued most were intelligence, knowledge, and authenticity rather than follower count or aesthetics.

Educational and “explain this to me” content has grown rapidly across TikTok and YouTube. Searches for “how does this work,” “explained,” and “day in the life” formats continue to rise. Recent creator economy research indicates that audiences are spending more time with niche experts who provide practical knowledge rather than aspirational imagery.

The algorithmic layer reinforces this shift. Content that demonstrates domain clarity and topic consistency is easier to classify and distribute than generalized lifestyle posting. Systems reward coherence.

professionals explaining their work,
scientists documenting field research,
policy staff sharing context,
nonprofit leaders walking through decisions,
engineers breaking down systems,
local organizers offering lived insight.

Mission-driven organizations have structural advantage here. Their teams already hold expertise. Their work already intersects with public questions. When that expertise is surfaced clearly, it travels differently than branded aspiration. The market is crowded with performance. It is less crowded with fluency.

What the Data Shows About Compression

When publishing accelerates at this level, platform-wide view totals are less revealing than per-post performance.

Metricool’s 2025 analysis of nearly six million short-form videos across TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, and Facebook Reels in 2025 show consistent tightening in reach per post. The data I refer to spans 2023 through 2025 and reflects a progression that began in the early 2020s as vertical video adoption accelerated across platforms. Publishing volume has increased faster than average exposure per post.

Operating under the theory that short-form consumption behaves as a cross-platform market, increased production and distribution shifts on one platform can affect attention patterns on another. If short-form output surges on TikTok while Instagram adjusts distribution, or if YouTube Shorts scales aggressively while Reels absorbs migration, audience consumption habits do not reset per app. They compound. In that environment, exposure thresholds rise and retention standards tighten across the ecosystem, not just within a single feed.

YouTube Shorts

On YouTube, short-form video feels like a spark cast into a much older fire, where visibility ignites quickly but authority builds through time, depth, and sustained attention.

  • Average brand publishing frequency exceeds 16 Shorts per month

  • Likes declined approximately 47 percent year over year

  • Engagement dropped by 36 percent

  • Reach decreased by 31 percent

TikTok

On TikTok, the feed moves like a current, and only content that grips within the first breath is carried outward into scale.

  • Publishing volume increased by more than 150 percent

  • Average reach declined roughly 47 percent

  • Engagement rate remains near 8.24 percent

  • Average watch duration is approximately 3.75 seconds

  • Average video length is around 41 seconds

  • Full completion rates hover near 4 percent

Instagram Reels

On Instagram, short-form video circulates inside a social web shaped by familiarity, identity, and habit, where distribution responds to behavioral signals more than volume alone.

  • Publishing volume increased over 34 percent

  • Larger accounts experienced substantial view declines

  • Smaller accounts under 1,000 followers saw improved reach and interactions

Facebook Reels

On Facebook, short-form video moves through a system still redistributing attention, where reach can stretch wider because the density has not yet hardened.

  • Views increased by approximately 24 percent

  • Interactions rose over 22 percent

  • Highest average reach per video among major platforms

LinkedIn

On LinkedIn, video travels along lines of professional proximity, and its reach expands when early engagement signals confirm relevance within trusted networks.

  • Video uploads increased by 20 percent in 2024

  • Video views increased by 34 percent in 2024

Short-form video has multiplied faster than attention can expand, and the compression becomes visible when you look at what happens to each individual post.

Oversaturation Is a Distribution Constraint

Feeds operate within finite session time. As the number of eligible posts expands, recommendation systems tighten exposure criteria: watch duration thresholds rise, early engagement velocity carries more weight, and topic consistency strengthens classification.

Short-form video now competes inside its own density. Increasing output inside a compressed system rarely restores reach unless clarity increases alongside it.

This is where the expert influencer has taken over. Content anchored in real expertise holds attention longer. It produces saves. It invites comments framed as questions rather than reactions. These behaviors feed distribution loops more effectively than passive scrolling.

In early 2026, many creators began reporting stronger performance from carousel posts relative to single-image posts and, in some cases, relative to Reels. On LinkedIn, concise commentary, document uploads, and insider reflections have gained noticeable traction in creator reporting and industry analysis.

As short-form publishing density increases, platforms distribute attention across formats that create varied interaction rhythms. What appears as a format resurgence is likely retention optimization inside a saturated system.

Why Image Formats Could Be Gaining Strength

In early 2026, many creators began reporting stronger performance from carousel and memes, in some cases, relative to short-form. On LinkedIn, memes, concise commentary, document uploads, and carousels have gained noticeable traction in creator reporting and industry analysis.

There is not yet sufficient platform-level data to confirm a structural shift toward image formats. What may be happening instead is retention optimization inside a saturated system. Swipe-based posts extend dwell time through layered information. Document uploads and carousels generate saves. Memes invite discussion without requiring continuous video completion. As short-form publishing density increases, platforms appear to be distributing attention across formats that create varied interaction rhythms. If Instagram’s algorithm currently classifies and distributes carousels more efficiently within its existing engagement architecture, that alone could account for the performance creators are observing.

The Post-Sale Shock and Instagram’s Aesthetic Constraint

Immediately following TikTok’s U.S. ownership transition in early 2026, creators reported sharp reach declines, search interruptions, inconsistent visibility, and noticeable shifts in how content surfaced. Major outlets documented instability during the transition, while the company attributed disruptions to infrastructure and technical changes. Whatever the cause, distribution patterns changed abruptly. For creators and nonprofits who rely on predictable visibility, that kind of algorithmic volatility functions as suppression in practice because it interrupts planning, forecasting, and revenue assumptions. As confidence eroded, cross-posting intensified and teams diversified toward Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts to reduce platform dependency.

Instagram absorbed that migration within a structurally different attention system. TikTok’s recommendation engine historically prioritized behavioral discovery, pushing content beyond follower networks when watch time and interaction patterns signaled interest. Instagram developed around social graph proximity and visual coherence, where identity signaling and aesthetic norms continue to shape interaction.

As expert-led and informational short-form video migrated into Instagram’s ecosystem, publishing density increased without proportional expansion in distribution capacity. If short-form production shifts across platforms, audiences may also experience changes in the type and quality of video they are shown.

When algorithmic matching becomes less precise or more crowded, engagement fatigue can follow. In that environment, carousels and memes may gain strength not as a rejection of video, but as a response to how Instagram distributes and surfaces content within its own structural logic.

AI Search and the Authority Layer

Short-form video expanded at the same time discovery began shifting toward conversational interfaces. Users now ask full, contextual questions inside AI systems and receive synthesized responses rather than scrolling through links. AI Overviews and chat-based answers cite a limited number of sources per query, concentrating visibility among content that is structured, attributable, and semantically clear.

Clarity and terminology now influence discoverability beyond the feed. Systems rely on transcripts, structured language, consistent domain framing, and retrievable context to generate answers. Content that can be summarized cleanly and mapped to a defined topic is more likely to be surfaced in AI responses.

Expert-led short-form video contributes to awareness, but authority accumulates in content that can be indexed, cited, and retrieved. Mission-driven organizations that articulate their expertise consistently across captions, transcripts, blog posts, and FAQs strengthen their presence in this emerging search layer.

YouTube, Long-Form Depth, and the Short-to-Long Pipeline

YouTube occupies a distinct position in this shift because its infrastructure is built around searchable transcripts, metadata depth, and session watch time. Shorts generate awareness, but long-form video builds sustained authority and search longevity. When short-form content is connected to deeper explainers, interviews, or panel discussions, organizations create a pipeline rather than isolated posts.

For nonprofits navigating short-form saturation, YouTube can function as a second home rather than a mirror. Cross-posting Shorts extends reach, but pairing them with long-form breakdowns strengthens retention, AI retrievability, and domain credibility. A short-to-long pipeline allows teams to capture attention in vertical feeds and then consolidate authority in structured, searchable depth.

In an environment where distribution compresses and AI systems prioritize clarity, organizations that connect short-form energy to long-form substance are better positioned to sustain visibility over time.

What Nonprofits Can Do Now

Short-form video remains valuable. The strategy must reflect both feed density and search evolution.

Clarify the Expertise

Define the specific domain your organization should be associated with. Encourage staff who hold that expertise to speak directly to it.

Anchor Content in Recognizable Structures

Use formats that audiences already understand. “Day in the life,” insider walkthroughs, quick policy explainers, and contextual commentary provide clarity for both viewers and algorithms.

Integrate Production Into Real Work

Film during existing meetings, site visits, interviews, and events. Authentic context increases credibility and sustainability.

Connect Short Form to Depth

Link videos to structured blog posts, reports, or transcripts. This strengthens AI retrievability and reinforces authority.

Maintain Terminology Consistency

Use aligned language across captions, transcripts, newsletters, and web content. Semantic alignment improves classification.

Measure Retention Signals

Track watch duration, saves, and shares. These behaviors influence distribution more than raw impressions in dense ecosystems.

Publish on a Sustainable Rhythm

Consistency builds recognition. Recognition strengthens distribution loops.

Is the Short-Form Video Market Oversaturated?

Publishing density has surged, filling the feed with more content than attention can sustain. Exposure per post has tightened across major platforms, and visibility no longer flows as easily as it once did. Trust is settling around people who know what they are talking about. Authority is accumulating where language is precise and ideas are structured clearly enough for both humans and machines to understand.

Short-form video still anchors digital communication, but it now lives inside systems shaped by retention, prediction, and AI classification. The terrain has shifted. The advantage belongs to organizations that speak with fluency, maintain coherence across formats, and connect short bursts of attention to deeper reservoirs of knowledge. That is where mission-driven work can claim ground and lead
intro_opening: What began on a single app that penetrated culture so deeply the format borrowed its name now anchors digital communication across nearly every major platform.
intro_closing: The advantage belongs to organizations that speak with fluency, maintain coherence across formats, and connect short bursts of attention to deeper reservoirs of knowledge.

title: Are hashtags dead?
excerpt: The hashtag was first proposed on Twitter as a way to group conversations. It spread because it solved a structural problem: how to index topics at scale across millions…
body_html:
The hashtag was first proposed on Twitter as a way to group conversations. It spread because it solved a structural problem: how to index topics at scale across millions of users. A post could attach itself to a larger conversation simply by labeling itself.

Hashtags were visible metadata. They allowed decentralized discovery. They created searchable clusters across people who did not follow one another. On early Instagram, you could browse chronological hashtag feeds, you could follow hashtags and receive posts outside your immediate network; discovery was topic-led and user-organized.

Hashtags are still present across platforms, but they no longer function as the engine of discovery. The infrastructure beneath them has shifted.

From User-Labeled Discovery to Machine Classification

Hashtags were once the primary way platforms understood what a post was about.

Today, platforms analyze far more signals than a tag appended to the end of a caption. Natural language processing evaluates the full caption. Computer vision interprets visual elements. Audio recognition parses spoken words. Transcripts are indexed. Watch time and retention are measured at the millisecond level.

Classification now happens at the system layer.

Instead of users organizing the internet through tags, platforms organize content through predictive modeling. Posts are routed based on probability:

  • who is likely to engage,

  • who has engaged with similar topics before,

  • how quickly others respond,

  • and how long viewers stay.

Hashtags have been absorbed into a larger architecture.

Instagram

The Quiet Demotion of the Hashtag

Instagram once relied heavily on hashtag feeds for discovery. Creators stacked ten, fifteen, sometimes thirty tags in hopes of expanding reach. Entire strategies were built around “hashtag research.”

Between 2020 and 2024, Instagram transitioned fully into recommendation-driven feeds. The Explore page, suggested Reels, and For You-style recommendations became primary discovery surfaces.

Public statements from Head of Instagram, Adam Mosseri solidified that hashtags do not meaningfully increase reach on Threads. In late 2025, Instagram capped hashtags at five per post. Although many creators report effective limits closer to three, and the ability to follow hashtags was removed.

Meta framed the change as an effort to reduce spam and emphasize meaningful tags over long lists of generic keywords. Internal testing and creator reporting suggest that posts with zero hashtags and posts with a few targeted hashtags perform similarly when engagement signals are equal.

Instagram now prioritizes:

  • Predicted interest based on user behavior

  • Watch time and retention on Reels

  • Saves and shares

  • Topic consistency across posts

  • Clear caption language

Hashtags now act as signals of context rather than engines of reach. They help clarify what a post is about, but Instagram’s classification systems increasingly read the entire caption for meaning. Investing in richer, more intentional caption language often strengthens discoverability more than adding additional tags.

LinkedIn

Relevance and Early Engagement Velocity

LinkedIn’s feed is structured around predicted relevance. According to LinkedIn Engineering’s blog, “Understanding dwell time to improve LinkedIn feed ranking,” the feed ranking system uses machine learning models to estimate the probability that a member will click, react, comment, or share a post, along with expected downstream and upstream network effects. Those predictions are combined into a weighted score that determines how candidate updates are ordered in the feed.

LinkedIn’s Help Center states that hashtags “improve post visibility” by connecting content to relevant topics and audiences and recommends using three to five hashtags per post for discoverability. Within the platform’s guidance, hashtags function as topical connectors that help classify content inside professional conversations.

At the same time, LinkedIn’s ranking system prioritizes behavioral signals. Posts are evaluated based on how quickly they generate comments, reactions, and meaningful engagement within first-degree networks. LinkedIn’s Engineering blog explains that LinkedIn introduced dwell time modeling and a “P(skip)” model to reduce the visibility of updates that members quickly scroll past. Visibility expands when early response patterns indicate relevance and value.

Within platform guidance, hashtags function as topical connectors that help categorize content. But feed ranking itself is governed by behavioral prediction. On LinkedIn, visibility is primarily shaped by:

  • Relevance to your first-degree network

  • Early comment velocity

  • Predicted engagement probability

  • Dwell time and skip likelihood

  • Historic interaction patterns

Hashtags support contextual categorization within the visible layer of the platform. Distribution is determined more heavily by engagement signals and relevance modeling within LinkedIn’s ranking infrastructure.

TikTok

Retention Over Tagging

TikTok popularized trend-based tagging. Hashtags once acted as entry points into challenges and viral formats.

Today, TikTok recommends using up to five hashtags per post, but distribution is not unlocked at the tagging layer. The platform reads full captions, spoken words, on-screen text, and behavioral signals to determine who should see a video. A longer caption within TikTok’s 2,200-character limit allows for clearer keyword signaling than five appended hashtags alone. Using five to ten intentional keywords that you already deploy across your website, newsletters, and other platforms creates stronger semantic consistency than stacking trending tags.

The primary driver of reach is retention. Platform data consistently shows that:

  • Completion rate strongly influences distribution

  • Average watch time relative to video length predicts scale

  • Early engagement velocity increases likelihood of broader testing

Research on TikTok performance shows that many viral videos fall under thirty seconds. Although the average video length is longer, viewer drop-off happens quickly, making early retention critical. TikTok’s distribution model favors content that sustains attention, with completion rate and watch time carrying more structural weight than hashtags. Tags can support context, but clarity, retention, and behavioral response determine scale.

YouTube and the Long Arc of Search

On YouTube, hashtags have always been secondary to metadata, transcripts, and watch time. The platform was built as a searchable video archive long before vertical short-form accelerated distribution.

YouTube Shorts has generated over 70 billion views per day since 2024. Yet YouTube’s recommendation and search systems remain deeply influenced by long-form watch time, session duration, subscriber behavior, and topical consistency across a channel’s library.

Unlike feed-only platforms, YouTube operates on two layers simultaneously: recommendation and search. Its infrastructure parses titles, descriptions, transcripts, and viewer behavior to understand both topic and depth. Videos are indexed not just by tags, but by full spoken language.

Search indexing on YouTube is shaped by:

  • Title clarity

  • Description structure

  • Transcripts

  • Viewer retention

  • Session watch time

Hashtags may appear in descriptions, but they function as peripheral markers inside a system that reads full semantic context. Shorts can generate awareness, yet authority compounds through structured long-form content that sustains watch time and signals expertise over time.

The Broader Pattern

Early social feeds organized conversation through hashtags. Users labeled posts and those labels created visible streams that others could browse. A tag determined where content gathered and how it was surfaced.

As content volume expanded into the billions, tagging alone could not organize attention. Systems began reading full captions, visuals, transcripts, audio, and engagement history to determine distribution. Meaning is now inferred from the entire post and from audience behavior around it.

Reach is shaped by:

  • Predicted interest modeling

  • Engagement velocity

  • Completion and retention

  • Semantic clarity

  • Consistency of positioning

Content circulates when models calculate sustained relevance and interaction probability. Hashtags can contribute context, yet distribution is governed by predictive infrastructure.

What Led Us Here?

Hashtag-led discovery worked when feeds were chronological and networks were smaller. The hashtag functioned as a visible organizing layer. It created topic clusters that anyone could enter. It helped build the early architecture of social discovery by allowing users to label and group content at scale.

As platforms expanded to billions of users and trillions of posts, manual clustering became inefficient. Recommendation systems began operating beneath that visible layer, interpreting full captions, images, audio, and behavioral signals to determine how content should move. Distribution shifted from user-labeled grouping to predictive modeling. Classification moved into the foundation of the system.

Large Language Models accelerated this shift while also preserving an older pattern. Many generative tools were trained on versions of the internet where hashtags were treated as meaningful levers for reach. That training data still shapes how AI drafts social posts. As a result, automated content frequently includes long strings of generic or loosely related tags. The formatting reflects a previous growth strategy that no longer governs distribution.

This is how inflation entered the tagging layer. The volume of hashtags has increased while their structural influence has declined. The visible artifact remains, but the foundation that determines reach now operates elsewhere.

Hashtags helped build the architecture of early social media. The systems governing distribution today rest on different foundations, where language clarity and behavioral signals carry more weight than appended labels.

What This Means for Creators and Organizations

Hashtags do not meaningfully increase reach in 2026. Across major platforms, distribution is governed by predictive modeling, retention thresholds, engagement velocity, and semantic interpretation. Public statements from Instagram leadership confirm that hashtags do not significantly expand reach. LinkedIn positions them as topical labels. TikTok and YouTube prioritize watch time, completion rate, and audience response patterns. Platform behavior reflects this consistently.

A few relevant tags can clarify context. Large stacks of generic hashtags add visible volume without structural leverage because classification happens beneath the surface of the caption.

The levers that influence distribution now sit in the foundation of the system:

  • Clear positioning inside captions

  • Consistent topic architecture across posts

  • Early engagement from a defined audience

  • Retention and watch time

  • Language structured clearly enough for systems to interpret

Hashtags do have some visibility, but distribution is determined deeper in the infrastructure.

What Do Hashtags Symbolize Today?

As AI-assisted publishing has expanded, one of its most recognizable surface patterns is the dense hashtag block at the end of a caption. The formatting often appears standardized. The tag clusters repeat across industries. The categories are broad enough to apply almost anywhere. Over time, that repetition changes how the formatting is perceived.

Audiences are developing pattern recognition. Heavy hashtag use increasingly reads as templated production rather than authored voice.

My hot take is this: in 2026, excessive hashtag stacking has become a visual marker of AI-generated content. It signals optimization layered onto messaging. It suggests automation before expertise. Brands that continue to rely on visible stacks of tags risk gradual erosion of audience trust, not because hashtags are inherently flawed, but because the formatting now resembles machine output more than human fluency.

Trust consolidates around clarity and voice. Around language that stands on its own without visible padding.

Hashtags helped build an earlier layer of the social web. Today’s infrastructure operates elsewhere, and audiences can feel that shift. The question is not how many hashtags to add. The question is whether your language carries enough structural integrity to travel without them.

intro_closing: The question is whether your language carries enough structural integrity to travel without them.

title: How Search Is Changing in the Age of AI
excerpt: Instead of typing a few keywords into Google and scrolling through links, you’re more likely to ask a full question and expect a complete answer.
body_html:
Instead of typing a few keywords into Google and scrolling through links, you’re more likely to ask a full question and expect a complete answer.

You might paste a paragraph into ChatGPT and ask for clarity.

You might ask a tool to explain something  of opening five tabs and piecing it together yourself.

That shift is structural. And has consequences for how information is found, trusted, and shared online.

For years, discovery on the internet was shaped by search engines and keywords. Today, it is increasingly shaped by conversational systems that summarize, select, and present answers directly. Understanding that change matters, especially for nonprofits and mission-driven organizations that rely on visibility to do their work.

01From Keyword Search to Conversational Discovery

For a long time, search behavior was transactional. People typed short phrases like “food pantry near me” or “climate change statistics” and scanned a list of links. Websites were built to capture that behavior. Content was optimized around keywords, metadata, and ranking position.

Research from Backlinko shows that most traditional search queries contain three words or fewer. That reflected both how people searched and how search engines returned results.

Generative AI has shifted that behavior. People now ask questions the way they speak. They expect an answer that feels complete. Harvard Business Review reports that 65 percent of Gen Z adults use AI chatbots as a substitute for traditional search engines. Many of those users have become accustomed to conversational AI as a replacement for search, but lack literacy on AI hallucinations and error rate.

User expectations have changed the user experience. Discovery has become less about navigating links and more about receiving explanations.

02How AI Systems Decide What Gets Seen

Generative systems do not rank pages in the same way search engines do. They select sources they can summarize confidently and combine into a coherent response.

That means content is more likely to surface when it:

  • Answers a specific question clearly

  • Is structured in a way machines can parse

  • Signals credibility through consistency and clarity

  • Uses language that matches how people actually ask questions

This is why people are starting to describe a shift toward Generative Engine Optimization. The goal is to be useful enough that an AI system can rely on your content when generating an answer.

In practice, this favors content that is explanatory rather than promotional and precise rather than broad.

03Why YouTube Shows Up So Often in AI Answers

One of the quiet but important changes in this shift is the growing role of YouTube.

YouTube videos are highly accessible to AI systems because they come with transcripts, structured metadata, and natural language titles and descriptions. That makes them easy to parse and summarize.

Several industry studies have found that YouTube appears in roughly 30 percent of generative search overviews. It now shows up more often than Reddit as a quoted source in AI-generated answers.

This helps explain why explanatory video content performs so well in modern discovery systems. This plays a huge role in accessibility to machines that are tasked with summarizing the internet.

04Monetization Is Entering the Conversation Layer

Search has been shaped by money, and generative search will be no different.

OpenAI has begun testing ads in the free version of ChatGPT. These appear when user intent suggests a commercial query, and they are placed directly under generated answers. The structure mirrors how Google Ads function, but the placement happens inside the answer flow.

This raises important questions about how visibility will work as these systems scale. Organizations with large budgets will have more options to pay for placement. Others will depend more heavily on consistency, clarity, and trust to remain visible.

For mission-driven organizations, this makes content strategy more consequential. Visibility becomes harder to separate from power.

05What This Means for Nonprofits and Mission-Driven Brands

As discovery shifts toward conversational systems, a few strategic adjustments become especially important.

First, content needs to be designed around real questions. This means understanding what your audience is trying to learn. Tools like AnswerThePublic, Google’s “People Also Ask,” and direct community feedback can help surface those questions.

Second, video matters more than many teams expect. YouTube is a major knowledge source for AI systems. Clear, explanatory videos increase the chances that your work is referenced and shared through generative tools.

Third, websites should prioritize clarity over volume. Pages that answer one question well are more valuable than pages that try to cover everything. Clear headings, concise summaries, and accessible language help both humans and machines understand your work.

Finally, structure matters. Using FAQs, schema markup, and consistent page formats helps AI systems interpret your content accurately

06Why This Matters

When discovery happens through summaries, someone decides what gets included and what gets left out. That decision is shaped by what is easiest to explain, easiest to trust, and easiest to compress.

This has implications for equity, nuance, and power. Complex work that resists simplification risks being overlooked. Organizations that lack resources to experiment with new formats may struggle to stay visible.

At the same time, this shift rewards clarity, accessibility, and genuine usefulness. It creates space for organizations that are willing to explain their work plainly and meet people where they are.

The way people find information is changing. The question is how intentionally we respond to it.
